text: This paper reports the development of a gold(I)-catalyzed sulfination of aryl and heteroaryl boronic acids. While gold complexes are typically utilized for the activation of $\pi$-systems, this method leverages a redox-neutral catalytic cycle based on the fundamental organometallic reactivity of gold(I)-heteroatom bonds. Key steps in the mechanism include the transmetalation of boronic acids with gold(I) alkoxides—formed in situ from gold(I) halides and base—and the subsequent insertion of sulfur dioxide into the resulting gold(I)-carbon bond. Systematic stoichiometric studies and the isolation of key gold(I)-aryl and gold(I)-sulfinate intermediates support the proposed catalytic pathway. The reaction utilizes potassium metabisulfite as a convenient $SO_2$ source and is compatible with various electronically diverse boronic acids and medicinally relevant heterocycles. Furthermore, the versatility of the generated sulfinate intermediates is demonstrated through their in situ conversion into a library of sulfones and sulfonamides, providing a practical synthetic tool for the construction of important pharmacophores in drug discovery.
